metadata.title: Immunogenicity Assessment for Therapeutic Protein Products — Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must adopt a risk-based approach to evaluating and mitigating immune responses to therapeutic protein products; any approach to assessing and mitigating immunogenicity must be determined on a case-by-case basis and should take into account the risk assessment described in the guidance.
rules.source_section: Section I — Introduction
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA outlines and recommends adoption of a risk-based approach to evaluating and mitigating immune responses to or adverse immunologically related responses associated with therapeutic protein products that affect their safety and efficacy. Any given approach to assessing and mitigating immunogenicity is determined on a case-by-case basis and should take into account the risk assessment we describe.

rules.rule_text: The underlying immunologic mechanism for immunologically related adverse events must be elucidated during development to facilitate strategies to mitigate risk; the applicant must provide a rationale for the proposed immunogenicity testing paradigm based on product- and patient-specific concerns.
rules.source_section: Section III — Clinical Consequences
rules.verbatim_quote: During therapeutic protein product development, elucidation of a specific underlying immunologic mechanism for immunologically related adverse events is encouraged, because this information can facilitate the development of strategies to help mitigate their risk. The applicant should provide a rationale for the proposed immunogenicity testing paradigm, based on product- and patient-specific concerns.

rules.rule_text: All cases meeting the clinical diagnostic criteria of anaphylaxis must be identified in clinical development, regardless of the presumed pathophysiology; the definition based on clinical diagnostic criteria must be used rather than a mechanistic definition requiring identification of specific IgE antibodies.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1 — Anaphylaxis
rules.verbatim_quote: the Agency recommends identifying all cases meeting the clinical diagnostic criteria of anaphylaxis, regardless of the presumed pathophysiology.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must not categorize adverse events merely as 'infusion reactions' without further detail; more-descriptive terminology noting the timing, duration, and specific signs and symptoms observed must be used, along with data from mechanistic studies.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3 — Infusion Reactions
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors are encouraged to use more-descriptive terminology when possible, noting the timing, duration, and specific signs and symptoms observed upon administration of a therapeutic protein product and to provide data from mechanistic studies which may facilitate a mitigation strategy.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must consider the potential transmission of antibodies to the fetus via placenta or to the developing neonate via human milk when assessing the risk of products that are counterparts of endogenous proteins critical to fetal or neonatal development; the risk of neutralizing antibody development following administration to women of childbearing potential must be strongly considered.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.5 — Cross-Reactivity to Endogenous Proteins
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, the risk of neutralizing antibody development following administration of such therapeutic protein products to women of childbearing potential should be strongly considered in light of the potential benefit. Moreover, the risk of neutralizing antibody development to endogenous proteins critical in growth and development beyond the neonatal period should be evaluated in studies in pediatric populations.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must develop and implement sensitive immunoassays for anti-drug antibody (ADA) detection commensurate with the overall product development program; concomitant assessment of therapeutic protein product levels in the sample is recommended to assess the potential for the product to interfere with antibody detection in the assay.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Recommendations for Immunogenicity Risk Mitigation: Development of Assays for ADA
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should develop and implement sensitive immunoassays commensurate with the overall product development program. Concomitant assessment of levels of therapeutic protein product in the sample is recommended to assess the potential for the presence of the product to interfere with detection of antibody in the assay.
cross_references: FDA Draft Guidance: Assay Development for Immunogenicity Testing of Therapeutic Proteins

rules.rule_text: Baseline samples for ADA testing must be collected prior to administration of the therapeutic protein product; post-baseline sampling frequency and duration must reflect anticipated use, with more frequent sampling during initiation and early use of a new chronically administered product.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Product-specific antibody sampling considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Baseline samples for ADA testing should be collected, and the post-baseline sampling frequency and duration should reflect anticipated use of the product. More frequent sampling is appropriate during the initiation and early use of a new, chronically administered product; less frequent sampling may be appropriate after prolonged use. Samples for antibody assessment should be drawn prior to administration of the therapeutic protein product.
rules.exceptions: Less frequent sampling may be appropriate after prolonged use

rules.rule_text: Repeat ADA sampling must occur over periods of sufficient duration to determine whether antibody responses are persistent, neutralizing, and associated with clinical sequelae; unscheduled sampling triggered by suspected immunologically related adverse events is also necessary.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Product-specific antibody sampling considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Repeat sampling should generally occur over periods of sufficient duration to determine whether these responses are persistent, neutralizing, and associated with clinical sequelae. In addition to a prespecified sampling schedule, unscheduled sampling, triggered by suspected immunologically related adverse events, is necessary for establishing the clinical relevance of ADAs.

rules.rule_text: Banking of serum samples from clinical trials under appropriate storage conditions for future testing is always advisable.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Product-specific antibody sampling considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Banking of serum samples from clinical trials under appropriate storage conditions for future testing is always advisable.

rules.rule_text: For first-in-human trials, a conservative dosing approach in an appropriate medical setting with access to immediate supportive care must be taken; trial design must include prespecified dose escalation criteria and adequate time intervals between dosing cohorts.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Dosing
rules.verbatim_quote: For first-in-human trials, a conservative approach in an appropriate medical setting with access to immediate supportive care in the event of a serious adverse event, such as anaphylaxis, should be taken. Staggered dosing among individual patients and dosing cohorts is appropriate. The trial design should include prespecified dose escalation criteria and adequate time intervals between dosing cohorts and, as appropriate for the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of the product, between individuals within a dosing cohort to assess toxicities prior to administration of subsequent doses or treatment of additional individuals.
rules.exceptions: Other scenarios with similarly conservative approaches may be indicated: change in route of administration, change in formulation, change in container closure system
cross_references: FDA Guidance: Estimating the Maximum Safe Starting Dose in Initial Clinical Trials for Therapeutics in Adult Healthy Volunteers

rules.rule_text: A protocol defining specific safety monitoring evaluations and stopping rules must be developed prior to implementation of dose escalation strategies intended to overcome an antibody response, as higher doses do not uniformly overcome high titer and/or sustained or neutralizing antibody responses and may cause additional toxicities.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Dosing
rules.verbatim_quote: Higher doses of therapeutic protein products do not uniformly overcome high titer and/or sustained or neutralizing antibody responses and may impact safety, e.g., may precipitate immune complex mediated disease or cause other toxicities. A protocol defining specific safety monitoring evaluations and stopping rules should be developed prior to implementation of dose escalation to overcome an antibody response.

rules.rule_text: In high-risk situations (e.g., products with a nonredundant endogenous counterpart), real-time antibody assessments during a clinical trial may be recommended for safety reasons; these entail analyses of samples as soon as possible after sampling, before banking, and prior to additional dosing.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Adverse Events
rules.verbatim_quote: In certain high-risk situations (e.g., assessment of a product with a nonredundant endogenous counterpart), real-time assessments for antibodies during a clinical trial may be recommended for safety reasons. Real-time assessments entail analyses of the samples as soon as possible after sampling, before banking of the samples, and prior to additional dosing.
rules.exceptions: The need for such intensive monitoring will depend on individual circumstances

rules.rule_text: For all comparative immunogenicity studies, a strong rationale and, when possible, prespecified criteria must be provided to justify what differences in incidence or severity of immune responses would constitute an unacceptable difference in product safety; the same antibody assay must be used to enable valid comparisons.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Comparative immunogenicity studies
rules.verbatim_quote: For all comparative immunogenicity studies (e.g., those comparing immunologically related adverse events, antibody incidence, titer, or neutralizing activity to product pre- and post-manufacturing changes), a strong rationale and, when possible, prespecified criteria should be provided to justify what differences in incidence or severity of immune responses would constitute an unacceptable difference in product safety. The same antibody assay should be used to enable valid comparisons.

rules.rule_text: Robust postmarketing safety monitoring is required as an important component in ensuring the safety and effectiveness of therapeutic protein products; sponsors must consult with the appropriate FDA review division to discuss the proposed approach to postmarketing safety monitoring.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Postmarketing safety monitoring
rules.verbatim_quote: Robust postmarketing safety monitoring is an important component in ensuring the safety and effectiveness of therapeutic protein products. Because some aspects of postmarketing safety monitoring are product-specific, FDA encourages sponsors to consult with the appropriate FDA review division to discuss the sponsor's proposed approach to postmarketing safety monitoring.

rules.rule_text: A new risk assessment must be performed for each new patient population considered for treatment; caution is recommended when moving from one patient population to another.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Patient-Specific Factors That Affect Immunogenicity
rules.verbatim_quote: caution is recommended when moving from one patient population to another, and a new risk assessment should be performed for each new patient population considered for treatment.

rules.rule_text: In the development of therapeutic protein products, a rationale must be provided to support the selection of an appropriate study population, especially for first-in-human studies; the potential influence of concomitant medications on ADAs must be taken into consideration during all stages of clinical development.
rules.source_section: Section V.A.1 — Immunologic Status and Competence of the Patient: Recommendation
rules.verbatim_quote: In the development of therapeutic protein products, a rationale should be provided to support the selection of an appropriate study population, especially for first-in-human studies. The potential influence of concomitant medications on ADAs should be taken into consideration during all stages of clinical development.

rules.rule_text: Screening for a history of relevant allergies pertaining to the source material of the therapeutic protein product is recommended, and the appropriateness of additional clinical or laboratory tests prior to administration must be considered in light of the overall risk-benefit assessment.
rules.source_section: Section V.A.2 — Prior Sensitization/History of Allergy: Recommendation
rules.verbatim_quote: Screening for a history of relevant allergies pertaining to the source material of the therapeutic protein product (e.g., produced in transgenic hen eggs versus mammalian cells) is recommended, and the appropriateness of additional clinical or laboratory tests prior to administration should be considered in light of the overall risk-benefit assessment.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity must be considered when selecting the route of administration, especially for high-risk therapeutic protein products (e.g., therapeutic counterparts of nonredundant endogenous proteins) in first-in-human dosing; changes in route of administration or dosing during development require clinical safety data to support such changes.
rules.source_section: Section V.A.3 — Route of Administration, Dose, and Frequency: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: Immunogenicity should be considered when selecting an appropriate route of administration, especially for high-risk therapeutic protein products (e.g., therapeutic counterparts of nonredundant endogenous proteins) in first-in-human dosing. Changes in the route of administration or dosing during product development may be associated with changes in the immunogenicity profile, and clinical safety data should be obtained to support such changes.

rules.rule_text: Evaluation of genetic factors that may modulate the immune response to a therapeutic protein product is recommended in circumstances where a subset of treated patients lose the clinical benefit of treatment or experience severe adverse events; HLA mapping studies may help define a subset of the patient population at increased risk.
rules.source_section: Section V.A.4 — Genetic Status: Recommendation
rules.verbatim_quote: Evaluation of genetic factors that may modulate the immune response to a therapeutic protein product is recommended in circumstances in which a subset of treated patients lose the clinical benefit of treatment or experience severe adverse events.

rules.rule_text: For a therapeutic protein product that is a counterpart of an endogenous protein, the robustness of immune tolerance to the endogenous protein must be investigated before initiating a clinical trial, with evaluation particularly required if the study is first-in-human, if the endogenous protein has a nonredundant physiological function, and if immune responses have been detected in autoimmune diseases.
rules.source_section: Section V.A.5 — Status of Immune Tolerance to Endogenous Protein: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: For a therapeutic protein product that is a counterpart of an endogenous protein, the robustness of immune tolerance to the endogenous protein should be investigated before initiating a clinical trial and such evaluation should consider the following as preeminent risks: if the clinical study is a first-in-human use, if the endogenous protein has a nonredundant physiological function, and if immune responses to the endogenous protein have been detected in the context of autoimmune diseases.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity testing must be considered when changes are made to product-specific factors; all therapeutic protein products must be evaluated for their content of and immune responses directed to incidental product components, including proteins and nonprotein components.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Product-Specific Factors That Affect Immunogenicity
rules.verbatim_quote: Immunogenicity testing should be considered when changes are made to product-specific factors. All therapeutic protein products should be evaluated for their content of and immune responses directed to incidental product components, including proteins and nonprotein components.

rules.rule_text: A risk-based evaluation of potential immune responses to process- and product-related impurities must be performed; foreign proteins intended for therapeutic use must be evaluated for molecular regions that bear strong homology to endogenous human proteins, and when such homologies exist, assessment of antibodies to the homologous human protein must be made in addition to assessment of antibodies to the foreign therapeutic protein.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.1 — Product Origin: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: A risk-based evaluation of potential immune responses to such process- and product-related impurities should be performed, and a testing program should be designed based on this evaluation. Foreign proteins intended for therapeutic use should be evaluated for molecular regions that bear strong homology to endogenous human proteins. When such homologies exist, assessment of antibodies to the homologous human protein should be made in addition to assessment of antibodies to the foreign therapeutic protein.

rules.rule_text: The ADA response to fusion molecules or engineered versions of therapeutic protein products must utilize assays that are able to assess reactivity to the whole molecule as well as to its distinct components; careful consideration must be given to primary sequences chosen for development especially for counterparts of endogenous proteins in view of potential polymorphisms.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.2 — Primary Molecular Structure/Posttranslational Modifications: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: The ADA response to fusion molecules or engineered versions of therapeutic protein products should utilize assays that are able to assess reactivity to the whole molecule as well as to its distinct components. Immune responses directed to the intact protein product, but not reactive with either of the separate partner proteins, may be targeting novel epitopes in the fusion region.

rules.rule_text: Manufacturers must minimize protein aggregation to the extent possible; strategies to minimize aggregate formation must be developed as early as feasible; product expiration dating must take into account any increase in protein aggregates associated with protein denaturation or degradation during storage.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.3 — Quaternary Structure: Product Aggregates: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: It is critical for manufacturers of therapeutic protein products to minimize protein aggregation to the extent possible. Strategies to minimize aggregate formation should be developed as early as feasible in product development. [...] It is particularly important that product expiration dating take into account any increase in protein aggregates associated with protein denaturation or degradation during storage.

rules.rule_text: Multiple methods that individually or in combination enhance detection of protein aggregates must be employed to characterize distinct species of aggregates; the use of any single method for assessment of aggregates is not sufficient to provide a robust measure of protein aggregation.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.3 — Quaternary Structure: Product Aggregates: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: Furthermore, the use of any single method for assessment of aggregates is not sufficient to provide a robust measure of protein aggregation. [...] Methods that individually or in combination enhance detection of protein aggregates should be employed to characterize distinct species of aggregates in a product.

rules.rule_text: Assessment must be made of the range and levels of subvisible particles (2–10 microns) present in therapeutic protein products initially and over the course of the shelf life; sponsors must conduct a risk assessment of the impact of these particles on clinical performance and develop control and mitigation strategies.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.3 — Quaternary Structure: Product Aggregates: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: Assessment should be made of the range and levels of subvisible particles (2–10 microns) present in therapeutic protein products initially and over the course of the shelf life. [...] Sponsors should conduct a risk assessment of the impact of these particles on the clinical performance of the therapeutic protein product and develop control and mitigation strategies based on that assessment, when appropriate.

rules.rule_text: For proteins that are normally glycosylated, use of a cell substrate production system and appropriate manufacturing methods that glycosylate the therapeutic protein product in a nonimmunogenic manner is recommended; for pegylated therapeutic protein products, the ADA assay must be able to detect both the anti-protein antibodies and antibodies against the PEG moiety.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.4 — Glycosylation/Pegylation: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: For proteins that are normally glycosylated, use of a cell substrate production system and appropriate manufacturing methods that glycosylate the therapeutic protein product in a nonimmunogenic manner is recommended. For pegylated therapeutic protein products, the ADA assay should be able to detect both the anti-protein antibodies and antibodies against the PEG moiety.

rules.rule_text: Manufacturers must minimize the types and amounts of microbial or host-cell-related impurities with adjuvant activity (IIRMIs) in therapeutic protein products; assays to evaluate the types of IIRMIs present must be tailored to the relevant cell substrate and must have sensitivities to assess levels that may lead to clinically relevant immune responses.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.5 — Impurities with Adjuvant Activity: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: It is very important for manufacturers to minimize the types and amounts of such microbial or host-cell-related impurities in therapeutic protein products. Assays to evaluate the types of IIRMIs present should be tailored to the relevant cell substrate. Because even trace levels of IIRMIs can modify the immunogenicity of a therapeutic protein product, the assays used to detect them should have sensitivities to assess levels that may lead to clinically relevant immune responses.

rules.rule_text: The immunomodulatory properties of therapeutic protein products, their effects on immune responses to themselves, and their capacity to induce autoimmunity must be monitored from the earliest stages of product development; vaccination using live attenuated organisms must be avoided when the therapeutic protein product is immunosuppressive.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.6 — Immunomodulatory Properties: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: The immunomodulatory properties of therapeutic protein products, their effects on immune responses to themselves, and their capacity to induce autoimmunity should be monitored from the earliest stages of product development. Vaccination using live attenuated organisms should be avoided when the therapeutic protein product is immunosuppressive.
rules.exceptions: Updated vaccination status, compliant with local health care standards, is recommended for patients before administration of the therapeutic protein product

rules.rule_text: Excipients must be evaluated for their potential to prevent denaturation and degradation of therapeutic protein products during storage; interactions between excipients and therapeutic proteins must be carefully evaluated, especially in terms of co-aggregation or formation of protein-excipient adducts; excipient stability must be carefully considered when establishing product shelf life.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.7 — Formulation: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: Excipients should be evaluated for their potential to prevent denaturation and degradation of therapeutic protein products during storage. Interactions between excipients and therapeutic proteins should be carefully evaluated, especially in terms of co-aggregation or formation of protein-excipient adducts. Excipient stability should be carefully considered when establishing product shelf life.

rules.rule_text: Thorough analyses of leachables and extractables from container closure materials must be performed to evaluate their capacity to interact with and modify the therapeutic protein product; leachables must be evaluated for each therapeutic protein product under real-time storage conditions.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.8 — Container Closure Considerations: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should conduct a comprehensive extractables and leachables laboratory assessment using multiple analytical techniques to assess the attributes of the container-closure system that could interact with and degrade protein therapeutic products. [...] leachables must be evaluated for each therapeutic protein product in the context of its storage container under real-time storage conditions.

rules.rule_text: Patient educational materials must explicitly identify appropriate storage and handling conditions of the product as required under 21 CFR 201.57 and 201.80; appropriate temperature-controlled transport and storage must be ensured.
rules.source_section: Section V.B.9 — Product Custody: Recommendations
rules.verbatim_quote: Patient educational materials (e.g., FDA-approved patient labeling providing instructions for use as required under 21 CFR 201.57 and 201.80) should explicitly identify appropriate storage and handling conditions of the product. [...] Appropriate temperature-controlled transport and storage should be ensured.

rules.rule_text: If patients develop signs or symptoms suggestive of immune complex disease (non-acute reactions), appropriate laboratory assessments for circulating immune complexes and complement activation must be undertaken and administration of the therapeutic protein product must be suspended.
rules.source_section: Appendix A.3 — Non-Acute Immune Responses
rules.verbatim_quote: If patients develop signs or symptoms suggestive of immune complex disease, appropriate laboratory assessments for circulating immune complexes and complement activation should be undertaken; and the administration of the therapeutic protein product should be suspended.

rules.rule_text: For products given chronically, one year or more of immunogenicity data must generally be collected and evaluated unless a shorter duration can be scientifically justified; antibody responses, regardless of apparent clinical effect, must be serially followed until levels return to baseline or an alternative approach is discussed with the Agency.
rules.source_section: Appendix A.4 — Antibody Responses to Therapeutic Protein Products
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, for products given chronically, one year or more of immunogenicity data should be collected and evaluated unless a shorter duration can be scientifically justified. [...] In some circumstances, antibody responses, regardless of apparent clinical effect, should be serially followed until the levels return to baseline or an alternative approach is discussed with the Agency.
rules.exceptions: Shorter duration can be scientifically justified; longer-term evaluation may be warranted depending on frequency and severity

rules.rule_text: For patients who appear to lose efficacy of a therapeutic protein product regardless of treatment duration, an assessment must be undertaken to determine whether the loss of efficacy is antibody mediated.
rules.source_section: Appendix A.4 — Antibody Responses to Therapeutic Protein Products
rules.verbatim_quote: Moreover, for patients in whom a therapeutic protein product appears to lose efficacy, regardless of the duration of the treatment course, it is important that an assessment be undertaken to determine whether the loss of efficacy is antibody mediated.

rules.rule_text: For products with potential to induce cytokine release syndrome, a risk-based evaluation focused on mechanism of action and animal/in vitro results must be performed to determine the need for collection of pre- and post-dose cytokine levels; in vitro assessments of cellular activation including proliferation and cytokine release in human whole blood or peripheral blood mononuclear cells are recommended.
rules.source_section: Appendix A.2 — Cytokine Release Syndrome
rules.verbatim_quote: A risk-based evaluation, focused on the mechanism of action of the therapeutic protein product as well as results of animal and in vitro evaluations should be performed to determine the need for collection of pre- and post-dose cytokine levels in the early phase of clinical development. [...] In vitro assessments of cellular activation, including proliferation and cytokine release in human whole blood or peripheral blood mononuclear cells, are recommended.

rules.rule_text: When evaluation of cytokine release is included in animal testing, measurement of a cytokine panel that is as broad as possible and includes IL-2, IL-6, IFN-gamma, and TNF-alpha, as well as other relevant cytokines indicative of cytokine release syndrome, is recommended; such proposed animal studies must be discussed with FDA prior to initiation.
rules.source_section: Appendix A.2 — Cytokine Release Syndrome
rules.verbatim_quote: When evaluation of cytokine release is included in animal testing, measurement of a cytokine panel that is as broad as possible and includes IL-2, IL-6, IFN-γ, and TNF-α, as well as other relevant cytokines indicative of cytokine release syndrome, is recommended. Such proposed animal studies should be discussed with FDA prior to initiation.

rules.rule_text: For patients who develop neutralizing antibodies or are at very high risk of developing neutralizing antibodies to a lifesaving therapeutic protein product (e.g., CRIM-negative patients), consideration must be given to tolerance induction regimens in a prophylactic setting; careful safety monitoring must be undertaken throughout the duration of any such protocol.
rules.source_section: Appendix A.4 — Antibody Responses to Therapeutic Protein Products
rules.verbatim_quote: For patients who develop neutralizing antibodies or are considered at very high risk of developing neutralizing antibodies to a lifesaving therapeutic protein product (e.g., CRIM-negative patients with a deletion mutation for a critical enzyme who are given enzyme replacement therapy), consideration should be given to tolerance induction regimens in a prophylactic setting, before or concomitant with the onset of treatment. [...] careful safety monitoring should be undertaken throughout the duration of the protocol.
